play_description: Defender trips the attacker from behind while challenging for the ball.

analysis.play_observation: Defender trips the attacker from behind while challenging for the ball.
analysis.reasoning: The retrieved 12.1 Direct free kick chunk lists tripping or attempting to trip an opponent as a direct free kick offence when committed carelessly, recklessly, or with excessive force. A referee call of foul is therefore supported by Law 12. The description may also require disciplinary review under 12.4 Serious foul play if the challenge endangered the opponent.

relevant_rules.text: A direct free kick is awarded if a player commits any of the following offences against an opponent in a manner considered by the referee to be careless, reckless or using excessive force: charges; jumps at; kicks or attempts to kick; pushes; strikes or attempts to strike, including head-butt; tackles or challenges; trips or attempts to trip. If an offence involves contact, it is penalised by a direct free kick. Careless is when a player shows a lack of attention or consideration when making a challenge or acts without precaution. Reckless is when a player acts with disregard to the danger to, or consequences for, an opponent and must be cautioned. Using excessive force is when a player exceeds the necessary use of force and/or endangers the safety of an opponent and must be sent off.

A tackle or challenge that endangers the safety of an opponent or uses excessive force or brutality must be sanctioned as serious foul play. A player who lunges at an opponent in challenging for the ball from the front, side or behind using one or both legs, with excessive force or endangering the safety of an opponent is guilty of serious foul play.

Violent conduct is when a player uses or attempts to use excessive force or brutality against an opponent when not challenging for the ball, or against any other person, regardless of whether contact is made. A player who, when not challenging for the ball, deliberately strikes someone on the head or face with the hand or arm is guilty of violent conduct unless the force used was negligible.
Direct and indirect free kicks and penalty kicks can only be awarded for offences committed when the ball is in play.
